transformation_type: olefination of carbonyl compounds to alkenes
mechanism_type: phosphorus ylide addition to carbonyl via betaine / oxaphosphetane
common_conditions: generation of ylide with strong base followed by addition to aldehyde or ketone at low to moderate temperature
description_short: Carbonyl olefination using phosphonium ylides to form alkenes, with stereoselectivity controlled by ylide stabilization, solvent, and reaction conditions.

mechanism_type: lithiobetaine equilibration and controlled collapse under organolithium / lithium halide conditions
reactant_pattern_text: nonstabilized phosphonium ylide + aldehyde or ketone under Schlosser conditions
common_conditions: organolithium-mediated deprotonation and equilibration of betaines, then protonation / elimination to favor E-alkene formation
synonym_names: Schlosser modification of the Wittig reaction|Wittig-Schlosser reaction
description_short: Modified Wittig olefination that uses strongly basic organolithium conditions to redirect nonstabilized ylides toward E-alkene products.

transformation_type: base-induced ether rearrangement to alcohol products
mechanism_type: radical-pair 1,2-shift or concerted 2,3-sigmatropic rearrangement of alpha-alkoxy carbanions
reactant_pattern_text: aryl alkyl ether or allylic ether capable of generating an alpha-alkoxy carbanion
common_conditions: strong base at low temperature to generate alpha-alkoxy carbanions, followed by rearrangement and workup
description_short: Rearrangement chemistry of deprotonated ethers in which alpha-alkoxy carbanions undergo 1,2-benzyl shifts or 2,3-sigmatropic shifts to alcohol products.

mechanism_type: NBS-mediated radical chain bromination at allylic / benzylic positions
reactant_pattern_text: alkene or alkyl arene with allylic or benzylic C-H bond + NBS
common_conditions: NBS under heat or light with radical initiator to maintain low bromine concentration and avoid addition across the double bond
description_short: Selective radical bromination of allylic or benzylic positions using NBS under thermal or photochemical initiation.

mechanism_type: loss of nitrogen from alpha-diazoketone followed by 1,2-shift to a ketene
reactant_pattern_text: alpha-diazoketone generated from acid chloride, diazo transfer, or related precursor
common_conditions: thermal, photochemical, or metal-mediated activation of diazoketones followed by rearrangement and ketene capture
description_short: Conversion of alpha-diazoketones into ketenes with accompanying 1,2-shift, widely used for ring contraction and homologation chemistry.
